questions.question: Should agent creation include machine/provider selection?
questions.rationale: The machine is plumbing; the model dropdown can carry provider grouping and availability

questions.question: What happens when a user has two machines with the same provider?
questions.recommendation: Pool them with sticky-per-match routing and failover
questions.rationale: Connector rebuilds sessions from full history, so failover is already safe

questions.question: Keep the name Connections or rename to Machines?
questions.rationale: Accurate, already everywhere in nav/keys/code; renaming buys little

questions.question: Which model do Hermes/OpenClaw agents use?
questions.recommendation: Whatever the user's own tool is configured with
questions.rationale: Their value is their own learning/memory; forcing a model fights the platform

discovery.summary: Design settled in conversation on 2026-06-10: wireframes for /me/connections list+detail (provider toggles, recent activity, runner box), grouped availability-aware model dropdown in agent creation, dynamic turn routing with sticky-per-match + failover, agents lose connection_id.

answers.Which model do Hermes/OpenClaw agents us: Whatever the user's own Hermes/OpenClaw is configured with — their model, memory, and learning come along. No model dropdown for them. Their connector adapters are explicitly OUT of this run (follow-up run; needs live-install testing for Hermes session-id capture).
answers.Should agent creation include machine/pr: No. Agent = name + model + strategy. Model dropdown groups models by provider and only shows providers enabled on at least one machine; unavailable providers appear greyed out with a pointer to the connections page. Machine and provider setup live on the connections page only.
answers.What happens when a user has two machine: They form a pool: turns route to any live connection with the provider enabled, sticky per match while the pinned connection is alive, failing over otherwise. A new machine rebuilds the match session from full history (already supported by the connector's first-turn path).
answers.Keep the name Connections or rename to M: Keep Connections. A connection = one computer running the connector. Sub-line copy explains it; nickname becomes the machine name (e.g. Home Mac).

non_goals: Hermes/OpenClaw connector adapters (follow-up run; needs a live install to verify Hermes session-id capture)
Renaming Connections to Machines (keeping the Connections name)
Changes to the direct-MCP play path (docs/setup-mcp.md)
Leaderboard/match-page presentation changes for black-box (Hermes/OpenClaw) agents
acceptance_criteria: Creating a connection no longer asks for a provider; setup hands out one key + one script per machine
Connection detail shows per-provider toggles with CLI-detection status reported by the connector; a provider with live agents cannot be silently turned off
Agent creation = name + model (grouped by provider, availability-aware) + strategy; no machine or provider step
Turns route to a live connection with the agent's provider enabled, sticky per match, with failover when the pinned connection dies
Existing connections and agents migrate with no data loss and no stalled running games
A stopped connection warns when it strands agents (no other live connection covers their provider)

checklist.goal: Replace per-provider connections with machine-level connections: install the connector once per machine with one key, toggle AI providers on/off from the site, and detach agents from connections so turns route dynamically to any live machine that supports the agent's provider (sticky per match, with failover).
checklist.audience: Players who field agents across multiple AI providers. Today they must create one connection per provider, run one connector process per provider, and re-pick providers during agent creation.
checklist.constraints: Schema migration touches every existing connection and agent; running games must not stall. Turn routing is hot-path engine logic. Sticky-per-match routing is required for prompt-cache economics; failover is safe because the connector re-establishes sessions with full game history. Connector gains a capability handshake (report detected CLIs); server must send provider explicitly per turn instead of inferring from model-name prefixes. Feature Factory checkpoints run on Chris's local machine (codex/gemini CLIs are not available in the remote session that authored this spec).
